skill.name: prompt-repetition
skill.description: Repeats prompts for lightweight LLMs to improve accuracy on multiple-choice, context retrieval, and position-based tasks.
skill.summary: Applies controlled prompt repetition to improve lightweight model accuracy.
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: Static findings for shell execution, credential files, weak cryptography, reconnaissance, and network behavior are false positives from Markdown examples, dictionary key access, and reference links. No evidence found of subprocess execution, eval, credential access, filesystem access, or outbound network calls. The remaining risk is semantic: the transformer repeats full prompts, including tool instructions, which can amplify untrusted or unsafe prompt content.

medium_findings.title: Full Prompt Repetition Can Amplify Unsafe Instructions
medium_findings.description: The transformer repeats the entire prompt and the documentation explicitly states that tool call instructions are repeated in full. This does not execute tools by itself, but it can increase the salience of malicious prompt injection or duplicate tool requests when integrated before an LLM call.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly duplicates caller-provided prompt text, and the documentation confirms full repetition of tool instructions. The risk depends on deployment guardrails, so the finding is medium rather than high.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misidentified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The external command findings are Markdown code fences, inline code, Python examples, and prompt examples. No shell, Ruby backtick execution, subprocess, eval, or command invocation was found in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are documentation examples or Python snippets inside Markdown. The implementation file imports only dataclasses, typing, and re, and contains no command execution API.

low_findings.title: Dictionary Key Access Misidentified as Sensitive Key Files
low_findings.description: The sensitive file findings point to list(MODEL_CONTEXT_WINDOWS.keys()), which reads dictionary keys in memory. No certificate, private key, token, or secret file access was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both flagged locations use the Python keys() method on a local dictionary. There is no filesystem path, credential material, or file-open operation at those lines.

low_findings.title: Reference Links Misidentified as Network Behavior
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are citation links in a References section. They are not fetched by the skill and do not transmit data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The links appear only as Markdown references. The executable Python file has no HTTP client imports or network calls.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Reconnaissance Findings Are Unsupported
low_findings.description: No cryptographic operation or host reconnaissance command appears at the flagged lines. The cited content is skill description text, a usage table, and multiple-choice prompt examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no hash functions, ciphers, system probes, or environment inspection at these locations. The static labels do not match the surrounding semantics.

low_findings.title: Critical Combination Heuristic Is Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: No evidence found for the combined pattern of code execution, network access, and credential access. The heuristic appears to combine unrelated false positives from Markdown examples, reference links, and dictionary key access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scanner reported a dangerous combination, but the reviewed files contain no executable network or credential-access path. Confidence is slightly lower because this summarizes a heuristic rather than one concrete line.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unfiltered Prompt Duplication Before LLM Calls
dangerous_patterns.description: The wrapper transforms caller-provided prompt text before passing it to llm_fn. Deployments should avoid applying this skill to untrusted tool-control prompts unless policy checks run before and after repetition.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The wrapper behavior is explicit in code. The severity depends on how downstream agents interpret repeated instructions and whether tool policies are enforced.

content.value_statement: Lightweight models can miss key context in long or options-first prompts. This skill repeats prompts in a controlled way to improve attention and answer accuracy.
actual_capabilities: Repeats eligible prompts two times by default for lightweight models.
Uses three repetitions for prompts with slot, position, index, row, column, or item patterns.
Skips prompts that already contain the prompt-repetition marker.
Skips chain-of-thought style prompts based on configured text patterns.
Reduces repetition count when estimated tokens exceed the configured context budget.
Provides a wrapper for applying the transformation before an LLM call.
limitations: It does not verify whether repeated prompts improve accuracy for every task.
It can increase input token cost by repeating the prompt text.
It uses a simple token estimate, not a model-specific tokenizer.
It does not sanitize prompt injection or unsafe tool instructions.

use_cases.title: Improve Multiple-Choice Accuracy
use_cases.description: Repeat options-first questions so lightweight models can revisit choices after reading the question.

use_cases.title: Strengthen Context Retrieval
use_cases.description: Repeat compact knowledge prompts when the answer depends on details that appear before the question.

use_cases.title: Handle Position-Based Queries
use_cases.description: Use extra repetition for inventory, slot, row, and position prompts where exact item location matters.

prompt_templates.title: Repeat a Simple Prompt
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner use with a lightweight model
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply prompt repetition to this short prompt for a lightweight model: [paste prompt]. Keep the final answer format unchanged.

prompt_templates.title: Improve Context Question Answering
prompt_templates.scenario: Long context followed by a direct question
prompt_templates.prompt: Use prompt repetition for this context and question task. Preserve all context text and answer only the final question: [paste task].

prompt_templates.title: Optimize Position Lookup
prompt_templates.scenario: Inventory, table, or indexed list query
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply the position-task repetition strategy to this indexed prompt. Return the requested item or position only: [paste list and question].

prompt_templates.title: Assess Repetition Fit
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate whether prompt repetition should be applied to this task. Consider model type, token budget, CoT patterns, and tool instructions: [paste prompt].

output_examples.input: A lightweight model receives answer choices before the question and must reply with one letter.
output_examples.output: The skill repeats the full question block twice, then the model returns the selected letter.

output_examples.input: A prompt asks which item is in slot 25 of a long inventory list.
output_examples.output: The skill applies the position-task strategy and repeats the prompt three times before the model answers with the item name.

output_examples.input: A prompt asks the model to think step by step before answering.
output_examples.output: The skill detects a chain-of-thought pattern and leaves the prompt unchanged.

best_practices: Use it first on lightweight models where attention limits are more likely.
Measure accuracy, latency, and token cost before enabling it broadly.
Run safety and tool-use checks before and after prompt repetition.
anti_patterns: Do not apply it repeatedly when the marker already exists.
Do not use it to compensate for unclear or unsafe prompts.
Do not apply it blindly to prompts that include tool-control instructions.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It repeats eligible prompts so lightweight models can revisit important context before answering.

faq.question: Which tools does it support?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: When should I use it?
faq.answer: Use it for lightweight models, multiple-choice prompts, context questions, and position-based lookup tasks.

faq.question: When should I avoid it?
faq.answer: Avoid it for reasoning prompts, very long prompts, and prompts with unsafe tool instructions.

faq.question: Does it call external services?
faq.answer: No evidence found of network calls in the reviewed implementation.

faq.question: What is the main security concern?
faq.answer: It can amplify malicious or unsafe prompt text because it repeats the full prompt.
